meta.title: The Alphabet Table — Playable Case
meta.hubTagline: A design-and-packaging house in the giftable-premium gap — vendor-made, Aamara-designed, launching on Diwali.

hero.badge: Aamara Capital · Business Idea 01 of 11 · On the table
hero.sub: The one idea already discussed with Mridul — now a build plan. Take apart the competitors, make the operating calls, green-light the Diwali launch.

opening.lead: Indians already pay premium for a well-designed, well-packaged plate — Nicobar proved it without a rupee of outside money. The lane between mass and luxury is wide open.

stats.labelHtml: Good Earth's FY25 revenue on ~10 stores — proof a craft-luxury table house is a real, profitable business
Outside funding behind Nicobar's ~26 stores — this category is fundable without venture money
Markup headroom — ~₹200 ex-factory to ~₹1,000 retail; that gap is design + packaging EST.
Letters, A–Z — one decal program turns a monogram into a whole giftable range

imgband.title: Design is the product. Clay is the vendor's job.
imgband.sub: We never fire a kiln. We own the design, the monogram and the box — and outsource the making to India's ceramic clusters. That split is the whole business.

whitespace.lead: Ten real brands, plotted by price and how giftable/personalised they are. Hover any dot. The top-right corner — premium and monogrammable — is empty. That corner is the idea.

players.d: You are here — designed, monogrammed, giftable-premium

whitespace.sidebarHtml:
Reading the map.

Nicobar and Good Earth are the same family (Eicher Goodearth) — the clearest proof the category works. But both treat gifting as a side note.

Nobody owns premium + personalised gifting. That empty top-right is what we build, and Diwali is how we enter it.

hook.titleHtml: Take apart the ones that matter.
hook.lead: Nicobar and Good Earth are the same family — the exact playbook we're studying. Flip each card: what they do, and the one move we copy or beat.
hook.statusInitialTail: flip all five to finish the teardown.
hook.statusSolvedHtml: Teardown done. The lesson: in-house design + packaging + a no-discount discipline build a fundable table house — no VC required. Our edge on top: a monogram/alphabet range and Diwali gifting as the wedge nobody else is defending.

cases.name: Good Earth
cases.det: In-house design studio, ~10 experiential flagships, ₹2,000+ a piece, one story-led collection a year. Our move: borrow the story discipline, sit well below their price, and add the personalisation they never bothered with.

cases.name: Nicobar
cases.det: ~26 stores self-funded, ~40% e-comm, illustrated boxes + gift notes, ₹550–1,300 band, never discounts. Our move: copy the packaging craft and price discipline — but start DTC + gifting, not a store chain.

cases.name: Ikai Asai
cases.det: Doesn't own kilns — co-creates with studio potters and sells the design. Living proof the outsourced-design-house model works. Our move: same model, giftable price, a signature monogram line.

cases.name: Nestasia
cases.det: Wide SKU, sourced/imported, ₹360 entry, dogged by markup complaints. Our move: out-design and out-package at a premium they can't credibly reach — the gifting buyer wants a story, not a haul.

cases.name: FabIndia Home
cases.det: ₹99 entry, generic handcrafted, the category's price floor. Great scale, no gifting-premium identity. Our move: be everything they structurally aren't — specific, designed, personalised, boxed.

personas.lead: Each has a trigger, an objection, and one thing that closes them. Flip each card.

items.name: The Host, 38
items.trig: A monogrammed set for her own table and for housewarming gifts — something she can't find at Good Earth.
items.obj: “Why not just buy Good Earth or Nicobar?”
items.conv: Personalisation they don't offer + a giftable price + a box worth photographing.

items.name: The Diwali Gifter
items.trig: Buys hundreds of premium Diwali gifts every year — wants distinctive, meaningful, made-in-India, monogrammed.
items.obj: Reliability & consistency across a large multi-recipient order.
items.conv: A gifting concierge + the A–Z monogram + a “limited Diwali edition” story. This is our launch customer.

items.name: The Registry Buyer
items.trig: “A set with the couple's initial on it.” Gifting with meaning built in.
items.obj: Needs it to feel special, arrive perfect, look expensive.
items.conv: Build-a-set + the monogram + a premium presentation box.

items.name: The Boutique Buyer
items.trig: Wants a signature table identity without luxury pricing.
items.obj: Consistency and reorder lead time at service scale.
items.conv: Small-batch custom, decal branding, and a reliable single-vendor reorder path.

tycoon.lead: Four real build decisions. Pick one — then see what the sourcing reality says.

decisions.ctx: The moat lives here. Choose where design sits.

opts.t: Let the vendor design from their catalogue
opts.fb: Then you're selling their moulds, not a brand. Factories are production houses — their “custom” just means applying your logo to their existing shapes. The design has to be ours.

opts.t: Design + packaging in-house; outsource only the firing
opts.fb: Correct — that's the whole moat, and it's the Ikai Asai / Nicobar model. Hire a freelance ceramic designer; own the shapes, the glaze story and the box.

decisions.q: 2 · How do you put A–Z on the pieces?
decisions.ctx: The monogram alphabet needs a production method.

opts.t: A custom embossed mould for each letter
opts.fb: 26 moulds at ₹15k–60k each is a fortune before you've sold a cup. Save embossing for a few hero SKUs later. EST.

opts.t: Ceramic decals — one printed transfer per letter
opts.fb: Correct. Decals (Mulder India / K.B. Polychem) are cheap per unit with one setup per letter — the right way to run a monogram alphabet. EST.

opts.t: Spread orders across many factories for safety
opts.fb: At pilot scale you'll get no priority and inconsistent bodies and glazes. Concentrate first to earn attention and quality.

opts.t: One anchor vendor — we supply design, decals & packaging
opts.fb: Correct. Fix one capable OEM (Clay Craft or RNS Global for volume; BAFA Pottery for a hand-painted hero line), give them everything, hold quality tight. Add a second only when volume demands it.
Capex and inventory risk before the brand is proven. Nicobar didn't open with a chain — it compounded from one store.

opts.t: Route Mridul's annual Diwali gifting into a limited first run
opts.fb: Correct. A captive first order, a real deadline, real feedback, zero cold-start marketing. The Diwali gift becomes the brand's launch.

alphabet.lead: One decal program, twenty-six gifts. Each piece wears its own initial — A on the achaar jar, B on the bowl, C on the cup, M on the mug — and a single idea becomes a whole personalised range.
alphabet.note: Highlighted letters are the launch four — Achaar jar, Bowl, Cup and Mug — the set that ships this Diwali. The rest roll out as the range grows.

human.titleHtml: The margin is the design, not the clay.
human.lead: A plate costs about ₹200 to make and sells at about ₹1,000. That gap isn't markup for its own sake — it's the design, the monogram and the box. Own those and the moat is yours; the kiln is a vendor line item.

rows.label: Ex-factory cost — the vendor makes it

human.note: That ~5× is Nicobar's proven band, not a hope. The clay is commoditised and outsourced; design + packaging + brand are the only things a competitor can't copy — so they're the only things we keep in-house. EST.

quiz.lead: Five quick questions before we green-light.

opts: A cheaper kiln than everyone else
In-house design + packaging + brand — the making is outsourced
items.e: We never fire a kiln. Design, the monogram program and the packaging are kept in-house because they're the only things a competitor can't copy; production is a vendor line item.

items.q: What's the relationship between Nicobar and Good Earth?
opts: The same family, both under Eicher Goodearth — Nicobar bootstrapped
items.e: Good Earth (Anita Lal, 1996) and Nicobar (Simran Lal & Raul Rai, 2015) are one family. Nicobar built ~26 stores with zero outside funding — the category is fundable without VC.

items.q: Best way to put A–Z on the pieces?
opts: Ceramic decals — one setup per letter
items.e: Decals are cheap per unit with one setup per letter — the right economics for a 26-letter monogram range. Embossed moulds are for a few hero SKUs later.

opts: We do — in-house / freelance; vendors are production houses
A design bought off the shelf
items.e: Factories default to applying your logo to their existing shapes. A distinctive brand needs us to bring the design and pay for custom moulds/decals.

opts: Route Mridul's annual Diwali gifting into a limited first run
items.e: The Diwali gift is a captive first order with a real deadline and real feedback — the brand's launch, funded by spend that was happening anyway.

vote.prompt: You've seen the map, the teardown, the operating calls and the range. This one's already on the table — so the real call is how we start.
vote.yesMsg: Green-lit. Phase 1 (next 30 days): shortlist one anchor vendor (Clay Craft / RNS Global for volume, BAFA for a hand-painted hero line), commission a freelance ceramic designer, print the first monogram decals (A, B, C, M), and lock a limited Diwali run against Mridul's existing gifting budget. The gift is the launch.
vote.noMsg: Sensible. Start with a single hero set — one shape, four monogram letters, a premium box — sold to a warm circle for this Diwali before committing to a full range. Same wedge, smaller first bite.

footer.note1: This playable walk-through is the execution companion to Idea 01, reframed after the discussion with Mridul. The original v1 diligence dossier — the artisan-cluster sourcing thesis — is preserved unchanged; open it below.
footer.note2: Figures marked EST. are estimates. Vendor and supplier names are real, publicly-listed leads — starting points for diligence, not commitments.
